title: Raised paving slab
detail: At around 9.45pm on Saturday 31st August, my husband tripped on a raised paving slab when walking from St Oswalds Church to his car parked on Festival Square. The fall was very severe. Two young men came out of the Wynstay opposite and tried to lift him up. They couldn't get him up as he was in excruciating pain. So the men called 999, went to get blankets to make him more comfortable, and waited with him until the paramedics arrived. They assessed him and thought he'd broken his left hip. They managed to get him into the ambulance and took him to A&E at Royal Shrewsbury Hospital. He arrived at about 11pm and was assessed in the Emergency Centre, sent for an xray in the early hours of Sunday 1st September and operated on, a partial hip replacement, at around 3pm on the Sunday. He then spent 2 weeks in RSH followed by 12 days in the Orthopaedic Hospital on a rehab ward. He was discharged home on Wednesday 25th September with Shropshire Council's START Team visiting morning and evening to help with personal care. The Community Therapy Team are also involved with the OT providing various pieces of equipment to help, and the physio to help with exercises. Recovery is gong to be a long slow process.

title: Elderly person fall on blue painted road surface
detail: The blue painted road surface is very difficult for some people to see and distinguish where the edge of the pavement is. My elderly father in law (aged 81) fell off this pavement recently and landed very heavily on his ribs and arms. This was on Saturday evening (4th October) at about 9pm. He was walking from the direction of Cross St and chatting to his brother. After passing the zebra crossing on his right, he approached the corner of pavement where the blue painted surface starts, he did not see that this was the edge of the pavement and fell very heavily and directly onto his front. He was very winded and took several minutes to recover before he as able to stand up and continue the walk home. He has been advised that he probably has a cracked rib but it is not worth an x-ray to confirm.

title: Monkmoor Road, very uneven & extremely bumpy road surface
detail: The problem is not with the breaking up of tarmac but the extremely uneven surface. I'm not sure how well it would photograph. The road surface is so ridiculously uneven & so makes any drive up and down Monkmoor Road extremely bumpy. We live just of this road. There is no other way of us exiting from our home address other than to travel on that road. It's been a nightmare for years and years. We've been waiting and hoping that it would eventually come up on the council's list of repairs. I have worked for Oswestry Borough & Shropshire Council, and also a Parish Council, & have also worked for a road surfacing company, so have some experience of the pressures that are put on councils/councillors and funds etc. We have been trying to avoid adding to that pressure ourselves for years & years. It won't have done our cars any good in the many years we've lived here. But now I have poor health so also have to have my mobility scooter in the back of the car very often. I need this to give me some form of independence. But the scooter is also now getting battered about, as well as the car itself. We have kept hoping it would have come up naturally on the schedule of works ages ago. Other residents must be suffering, too. We are at the point now of thinking we must try to finally to get something done about it. We have thought of prepping a petition type letter and getting everyone to sign it etc...There are very many residents who will have to use this road. It is unavoidable for so many. But decided to contact the council first directly. We are hoping that the council will see (easily) how bad it is & understand how patient we have been & finally get instruction to do some work to level it out. I'd like the council to consider if it were their only access-road when leaving their home how they would feel.

title: Flooding due to lack of maintenance of waterways
detail: Every time we get heavy rain, the weir by the side of the road opposite School House diverts the water onto the road. This is because it is not maintained or routinely cleared of accumulated debris. The same comment applies to several of the roadside drains further downstream. The result is that the road outside our property becomes a raging torrent, as the photo shows, and this has happened three times in the last two months. The force and depth of the water stops all traffic and is so strong that it is dangerous to even attempt to walk in it, cutting several houses off. Now the road surface is being broken up by the force of the water, with large suspension-damaging potholes developing. Although our property is just high enough to avoid being flooded by this torrent, the same cannot be said of some living further down.

title: Holbache rd residents illegally blocking the road with cones to reserve parking spaces.
detail: I’ve been reporting this issue to you since April and have spoken to you by phone. Seemingly no action taken to date. Residents are using cones to block parking spaces on Holbache Rd. Oswestry. We all want to park by our houses but you can’t if someone else has parked there. So park elsewhere. Some residents are placing cones outside their home to reserve parking spaces outside their homes. It illegal according to the Highway Act and unfair on other road users. Recently a district nurse visiting a disabled resident could not park due to the cone warriors and an ambulance attending an Emergancy- cones blocked parking access. While I understand residents want to park, it’s not ok to block parking access. There aren’t any parking restrictions or double yellow lines or resident only parking arrangements. Since reporting this issue in April and May, it’s become a bit of a contagion as more residents are now coning. One day recently nearly all of one side of the street was coned off by residents, some using “Police” cones nicked following the recent balloon festival. title: Lack of appropriate and missing signage
detail:
It's been many months since my initial report, and I've not received any updates, so I wanted to highlight a few ongoing issues following a site visit.

🔸️Chirk Road needs to be 20MPH during school hours. Many families need to use Chirk Road to drop off and collect their children. As a result, a large amount of pupils are exiting school and needing to cross the road. Often between cars, as the road becomes very busy. There are no VAS signs despite funding being in place, no lighted signage to warn drivers they are entering a school zone, no proper crossing and no speed reducing measures in place. There really are a lot of options available to make the area safer for pupils and parents alike.

School lane is now missing it's 20MPH signage following my report of mismatched signage on either end of the lane. As a result, drivers are now unaware of the speed limit change and are driving down School Lane assuming it is 30MPH in line with the joining roads.

After standing on the junction I need to highlight that urgent action is required to bring the roads in line with the safety standards of other local schools. Please correspond at your earliest convenience.

Many thanks Cllr Jay Moore

title: Largest flytip site in Oswestry
detail:
I'm sure you are more than aware of the situation regarding flytipping at Crestwood Court, thanks to the sheer quantity of reports stretching back to 2023.

Since then, we have: Had several areas cleared. Funded and installed CCTV. Had the area confirmed as the worst for flytipping in Oswestry by Veolia. Liased with West Mercia Police, STAR Housing, Shropshire Fire Prevention team, residents, neighbours and more.

Residents are understandably furious with the situation, and it seems to just be delay after delay.

This area needs clearing immediately. It is now dry, and is a real fire hazard. I am struggling to find any word at this point other than neglect.

The Town Council is now spending money to try and tackle the issue. It is time Shropshire Council clear it, so residents can enjoy some time outside as the weather improves without having to live next to the rotting food waste and eye sore of dozens of mattresses and white goods.

Please clear it. It really needs putting to the top of the schedule now. It's been far too long and residents in the area have suffered for too long.

Kind regards, Cllr Jay Moore

title: Collapsing pavement and sinkhole
detail: The pavement in front of our house has started to sag and dip, and a sinkhole has started to form in the tarmac by our gate. The drains in the road were flooding lots at christmas and I reported them (you sent the gully sucker out in january). However since then the pavement has sagged and a sinkhole has appeared. I visited the council archives to try and figure out if any services pass under this section, and the road drains drain to a soakaway under our driveway apparently. My understanding is that despite the soakaway being within our land, its the council's responsibility to maintain it. I am now concerned that the soakaway isn't functioning properly, or the soakaway pipe may be blocked and this is having a knock-on effect for the stability of the surrounding area. The tarmac at the join for the pavement to our boundary wall is starting to crack too and I don't want there to be any issues with our driveway access or indeed the foundations of our home or stability of the wall, as the soakaway is from the 1960's and is located less than 5m from our house which isn't acceptable by modern standards. title: Carpark ownership
detail: Can someone tell me who owns the carpark that is accessed from Hawthorn grove in Oswestry please? Where the ground has been dug out has left a wall of hard soil that's about 18 inches high and I need to get to the back of my fence so that I can cut back the brambles and a fir hedge to stop them pushing through the wooden panels, I have balance problems so I can't get up and over the soil bank to maintain the rear of my fence. Also I would like to point out that the residents let their children play on the carpark and the constant screaming and shouting from 3.30pm till 8pm school days and all day on the weekends is driving my stress levels through the roof which is preventing me from wanting to spend any time in my garden. Not being able to drive and being on a low income means my garden is the only place I can get any real enjoyment. Hopefully a resolution can be found for both issues, thank you.

title: Cars parked close to entrance and along street with little to no visability to pull out of junction. RTC hazard
detail: Pulling out of Mount Drive is increasingly becoming an "accident waiting to happen". Cars parked along Mount Road (Mount Drive side) close to the junction of Miunt Drive, towards the direction of the racecourse are blocking any view of vehicles coming down Mount Road from the racecourse direction (as you can see from the picture), these vehicles do not tend to comply with the speed limit. In the opposite direction (heading into town) vehicles are again parked along Mount Road close to the juntion with Mount Drive, with limited visibility. One car in particular hasn't moved for over 6 months. Visibility is slightly better as you are "looking down" the road whereas towards the racecourse direction you are looking up the road and any car parked on that side restricts visibility. Coming out of Mount Drive you have to pull across one lane before being able to see if there on any oncoming vehicles coming down from the race course direction. This morning, when these photos were taken, I had to pull straight out of the drive and "hope for the best" as I couldn't see up the road due to the vehicles parked all the way up Mount Road or around the vehicle closest to the junction of Mount Drive. It is worse if the vehicle is a higher vehicle ie van. The vehicles tend to be people walking their dogs on the field next to the fire station or workers/visitors going into town. The road either needs double yellow lines along it or at least a mirror opposite Mount Drive so we can see vehicles coming.

detail: There is a road crossing between ALDI and the bus station where cars can drive into ALDI. A couple of months ago I was hit by a van and went to hospital with a fractured foot. You literally cannot see a thing and have to just pull out and hope no one is coming along Mount Drive. This is made even worse by the fact that just up from this junction, the speed limit goes from 60 to 30, meaning that most people are also still speeding when then pass the junction. All we need that would resolve this issue are two mirrors on the lamppost opposite the junction to give visibility in each direction. This matter is seriously urgent. The issue has been reported in the past but nothing was done. Me and my 9 month old baby were almost in a crash yesterday and it’s only a matter of time before someone crashes here. The matter has become urgent as of last week because there is building work being done on a house close by and there are a lot more vehicles parked on Mount Drive than there normally are. This building work is going to be going on for at least 6 months so the issue really needs addressing immediately before someone is hurt.

title: Serious risk from badly degraded footpath surface
detail:
The surfaced path has now deteriorated to an extremely unsafe state. This pathway MUST be now be repaired appropriately as a matter of urgency. Large sections of tarmac have broken away; numerous potholes are deep and very unsafe. Many residents on the Agnes Hunt Memorial Bungalows are wheelchair users with mobility difficulties and are unable to use the footpath as a result. The situation has now become extremely hazardous for the many vulnerable residents living here.

Under the Equality Act 2010, public authorities, including local councils, have a duty to ensure that public spaces and highways are accessible to all individuals, including those with disabilities. The current state of Agnes Hunt Memorial Bungalows footpath surfacing fails to meet these standards, effectively preventing wheelchair users from accessing essential services, local businesses, and community spaces and leaving vulnerable residents isolated as a result.

I kindly request that Shropshire Council takes immediate action to repair the potholes and all dropped kerbs, to ensure that the footpath meets necessary accessibility requirements. Please provide a response outlining the steps that will be taken and a timeline for when repairs will be carried out.

Should this issue remain unresolved, I will have no choice but to escalate the matter to the Local Government Ombudsman and the various Disability Rights organisations for further review.

I do appreciate your prompt attention to this matter and look forward to your response. Please feel free to contact me should you require further information. Many thanks

title: Encroachment of shrubs onto pedestrian pavement
detail:
We contacted Shropshire Council, with our concern of narrowing of pedestrian pavements about a year ago, particularly along a stretch of pavement (SY11 2HU) opposite Lidl Supermarket. Workmen attended to it fairly quickly, by just 'nipping' the ends of the sprawling greenery, which, of course, immediately encouraged further growth.

We write again, attaching 2 photos:- Victoria Road, opposite Lidl Supermarket, SY11 2HU, where the pavement is about 18" wide. No children's buggies or electric scooters are able to reach the traffic light system, enabling access to the supermarket, cemetery or the council estate further down Salop/Shrewsbury Road, used by school children, all of whom have to step out into the flow of traffic, coming from behind.

Salop Road, opposite Halfords, SY11 2RL, where a section of the pavement is also narrow, restricting access for people with buggies or electric scooters.

We had suggested that the owners of the these particular properties should be approached with a view to maintaining their hedges; but it seems as though the owner may actually be Shropshire County Council, who owns the triangular plot of land between Victoria Road and Salop Road.

Perhaps a robust 'hacking back' is what is required, to minimise repeated maintenance.

Kind regards,

Melvin and Carole Gough

title: Inadequate signage and cone placement and raised curb with non compliant sign
detail: At approximately 0415 this morning i was travelling out of Oswestry on the B4579 going past an area of development, as I looked right prior to entering the roundabout I heard a loud bang as my wheel hit a pedestrian crossing feature that juts out into the highway bursting my tyre and damaging my steering, the area has new street lights but we're unlit and the bollard to demarcate this is non-conforming to NRSWA, in the wet and dark the base of this pedestrian feature blends into the highway, if this feature has yet to be commissioned the cones that are currently there should guide you around it not into it, at a time when I can barely afford to put food on the table I now have to get my car fixed, I suggest that the TM at this location is inadequate, I imagine i'm not the first person to be effected by this new crossing and likely won't be the last unless something is changed. The angle at which the crossing sits in the road is poor and the curb is barely visible, the bollard has no illumination and no blue directional arrow to highlight the edge of the crossing, please look into this. Once I find out what damage has been done to my steering I will be forced to take legal action to cover the expense of any repairs required to my vehicle.

title: Light configuration problem: Pedestrian crossing signal not active when car lights are on red
detail:
There is a pedestrian light controlled crossing enabling pedestrians to cross the one way traffic from Beatrice Street continuing straight (onto Thomas Savin Road) or turning right (towards Oswald Road).

When the vehicle traffic is sat at the red light (enabling other traffic flows), it is safe for pedestrians to cross here. However throughout the red light phase here, the pedestrian indication continues to show red.

When the pedestristrian request button is pushed, pedestrians must wait for an entire sequence of traffic light changes in order to cross. Sometimes this means waiting 1 or 2 minutes longer than required to safely cross to/from the traffic island.

I believe this to be a traffic light configuration error, with the pedestrian lights not illuminating green when they could safely do so in phase with the traffic.

title: Grass bank has slipped right across the pavement.
detail: For months, the grass verge on Morda Bank going up from Morda Mill has slipped more and more onto the pavement and soil and mud is now almost right across the pavement. This is now dangerous for our daughters when pushing a pushchair along with her 4yr old to and from school. Today, the pushchair of one of the mothers walking in front caught on the verge which is now only approx 40cm from the road and pushchair veered into the road, her small daughter tripped at this point and stepped right into the road. We’ve walked this route for 30years and the pavement used to be approx 1m wide, but over the years the steep verge has slipped across the pavement to the point when you cant push a pushchair along this stretch at all safely. Also the wheels of tractors cutting the hedges over winter have squashed the verge down across the pavement. If it is not cleared back to original pavement width there could be a serious issue with pushchairs or children tripping into the road.

title: Potholes needing attention
detail:
I refer to report ref 14193468 which I reported on 10 Jan 2026. Thanks for your response indicating the problem is now fixed and closed. However, I cannot believe that repairs can be done and other issues yards away are totally ignored - surely you don't want me to report each individual pothole separately as you will be inundated! Common sense must prevail and any further issues noticed when carrying out repairs then they should be attended to so to avoid repeat visits to the same site/area. I would respectfully request you inspect the whole stretch from Red House Lane to the junction of Stoney Road/Gibraltar Lane and repair ALL potholes and areas of rough road surface and attend to them appropriately. Please report to me when complete. Thanks.

PS. How no one is appointed to inspect stretches of roads to identify these issues beggars belief as SCC know full well that the state of the roads is atrocious and repairs are not always properly done and workmen are re-visiting the same sites over again. Surely a methodical approach would prove a better way of addressing the problems by doing designated stretches one at a time rather than in this haphazard way. I often see workmen patching one or two potholes then they return a few days later to repair other potholes nearby. Where is the sense in that? Thanks for reading.

title: Heave/subsidence of the road surface in the vicinity of a manhole and a road gully on Old Post Office Lane on the eastern side of the lane opposite the property known as Bryn Celyn. If a 3 metre straight edge was placed on the road at this location, the tarmac around the manhole cover has risen and the area around the road gulley has sunk leaving a height differential of around 200mm.
detail: A localised pothole repair was undertaken by Shropshire Highways during 2025 but it requires more detailed investigation. The centre of the carriageway along the length of Old Post Office Lane heaved vertically following the installation of a new water main using a mole method by Severn Trent Water a number of years ago. As the roadside gully was lower to collect the surface water at this location, this exacerbated the surface level problem. The issue results in the wheels of vehicles "grabbing" to one side and could easily lead to an incident. Particularly when the highway also combines as a footpath and it could lead to pedestrians stumbling and falling over.

title: Standing water on Highway - no gullies to drain away to roadside ditches
detail: Earlier today there was a lot of surface water between Red House Lane and Stoney Road/Gibraltar Lane junction which could cause safety issues when vehicles negotiate to avoid large puddles which could easily hide potholes. At this particular point there are ditches on both sides of the road where the water could be drained to so easily. Can gullies be cut to drain off this excess water to avoid this problem - they would require to be quite substantial to avoid the problem of them being blocked when these large tractors which use this road frequently ride over the grass verges! It can be done and would alleviate a lot of the surface water problems and also help reduce the problem of ice in wintry conditions and water freezing and breaking down the repaired potholes which exist beneath the water. My comments apply to a lot of places in Shropshire and I would have thought that attention to this type of upkeep would reduce hugely the increasing issues which surface water produces. Why not address them sooner than later.

title: Road covered in mud and animal manure with road surface deteriorating

title: Removing a frequently overflowing bin
detail:
I am writing to raise a concern regarding a street bin in my local area which is frequently overflowing and causing ongoing issues.

The bin is regularly filled beyond capacity, leading to litter, animal excretion spilling onto the surrounding pavement and street. This not only creates an unsightly environment but may also pose hygiene concerns and attract pests. Despite routine collection schedules, the problem persists and appears to be a recurring issue.

Please note that the council employees only empty the top bit of the bin and more than half stays there, we asked one of the staff who was collecting today that why is this not emptied fully, he replied his job is to just take the top bit which is overflowed. It smells very bad in the surroundings and it is at the entrance road of the grocery store.

Given the frequency of this problem, I would like to request that the council consider removing the bin from this location and place it somewhere more appropriate.

I would appreciate it if you could investigate this matter and advise on any actions that can be taken to resolve it.

Thank you for your time and attention. I look forward to your response.

title: Bins at Whittington Castle are overflowing.
detail:
Dear Shropshire Council, I am writing to report an ongoing issue with the waste bins located at Whittington Castle.

All three bins have been overflowing for well over a week, and as of today (Wednesday 6th May 2026), all three bins remain completely full, with rubbish spilling out onto the surrounding grounds. The situation appears to be worsening rather than improving.

This is becoming a significant concern, as the accumulation of waste poses a risk of attracting rats and other pests. Such an infestation could have serious consequences for the local environment, including harm to the ducks in the area, as well as risks to dogs that are regularly walked around the grounds. Additionally, this is a popular location for families, including young children and babies, and the current state of the area raises clear health and safety concerns.

I enclose some photos of all 3 bins for your attention.

I would appreciate it if this matter could be addressed as soon as possible, with the bins emptied and a more frequent collection schedule considered if necessary. Also I believe that having more bins located among the car park and around surrounding grounds will help keep on top of the ongoing litter issues.

Thank you for your attention to this issue. I look forward to your response.

Yours faithfully, Miss Tara Jane Goddard.

title: Overflowing Bins and Waste Management Issues at Whittington Castle
detail:
Dear Shropshire Council, I am writing to report a concerning issue regarding the waste bins at Whittington Castle.

The bins at the site have been overflowing for well over a week, and as of today, 6th May 2026, all three bins remain completely full. Rubbish is spilling out onto the surrounding ground, and there is also a significant amount of dog mess left unattended in the area. This is not only unsightly but also poses a serious hygiene concern.

The current situation creates a real risk of attracting rats, which could lead to an infestation. This would have serious consequences for the local environment, including harm to ducks and other wildlife, as well as risks to dogs that are regularly walked in the area. Additionally, families, children, and babies frequently visit and walk around the castle grounds, and their health and safety could be impacted.

Furthermore, a potential rat infestation would be particularly damaging to the kitchen facilities at Whittington Castle, which have only recently reopened. Maintaining a clean and safe environment is essential for its continued operation.

To help address this issue, I would strongly suggest: ■ Increasing the number of waste bins around the castle grounds ■ Introducing more frequent (ideally weekly) waste collections ■ Ensuring regular monitoring and maintenance of the existing bins

These measures would greatly benefit the public and help preserve the cleanliness and safety of this well-loved local site.

I would appreciate it if this matter could be addressed as soon as possible.

Thank you for your attention.

Yours faithfully, Miss Tara Jane Goddard.

title: Request for Inspection and Management of Trees Adjacent to Residential Property
detail:
Dear Sir/Madam,

I am writing to request that the council inspect several trees located on the public green space directly adjacent to my property at 4 Bryn Glas, Oswestry.

The trees include an English Oak, Norway Maple, European Beech, and Common Hawthorn. I have attached photographs showing their close proximity to neighbouring homes and boundary fencing.

Over recent years these trees have continued to increase in height and canopy spread, and I am becoming increasingly concerned about the potential risks they may pose if left unmanaged.

My concerns include:

• Branches and canopies extending very close to residential properties.

• The long-term suitability of large species such as the Oak, Norway Maple, and Beech in such close proximity to houses.

• Potential root impact on foundations, drainage, underground services, and nearby structures as the trees continue to mature.

• The risk of falling branches or structural failure during periods of severe weather or high winds.

• One tree appears to have a noticeable lean and may require professional assessment.

I appreciate the environmental value of trees; however, I believe these trees now require careful management to ensure they remain safe and appropriate for their location within a residential area.

I respectfully request that the council arrange a site inspection by a qualified arboricultural officer and consider any necessary remedial works, including pruning, crown reduction, ongoing maintenance, or removal where appropriate.

Please confirm that this matter has been logged and advise what action the council intends to take.

Yours faithfully, Elen Davies

title: Fatal Accident History & Road Safety Intervention Request
detail:
This section of road currently carries a default 60 mph National Speed Limit allowance, which ignores basic physics, the severe layout of the road, and a known history of severe and fatal accidents. This corner presents three compounding, critical hazards: 1. Extreme Braking Limitations: On this 17% descent, gravity heavily works against a vehicle’s brakes. According to Rule 126 of the Highway Code, stopping distances must be drastically increased on hills. When the road surface is wet, physics dictates that a vehicle traveling near the legal 60 mph threshold requires an estimated 241 metres to come to a stop—more than three times the standard baseline.

2. Terrain and the Sheer Drop: The physical geometry of this sweeping curve is completely blind on approach. Crucially, there is an unprotected sheer drop directly behind the perimeter of the corner. Any vehicle that fails to negotiate this turn due to excessive speed or a loss of traction faces a catastrophic fall, making any roadway departure inherently life-threatening.

3. Documented History of Serious and Fatal Crashes: This is not a theoretical hazard. This specific stretch of the B4580 past the Racecourse has seen recurring, severe incidents where vehicles have left the tarmac. This includes documented emergency turnouts involving cars landing upside down in fields, casualties requiring air ambulance extraction, and tragic fatalities. The status quo is actively costing lives.

Motorists simply cannot safely negotiate this sharp curve at the current legal speed threshold.

title: High Fawr Avenue - potholes and unsealed road surface
detail: High Fawr Avenue was ''closed' for three days and supposedly treated. It DOES NOT have the same appearance as other roads which were treated at the same time - it appears that it was not sealed by contractors. The potholes in the road were not filled before new gravel was distributed, so the road surface is now worse than it was before remedial work was supposedly undertaken. This applies to the entire length of High Fawr Avenue. On returning home, the wheels of our car have gravel embedded in them. I telephoned the pothole hotline on May 18th to discuss the potholes and was told that 'perhaps' the contractors would fill the hoes before sealing. The potholes were not treated. Sealing has not taken place, as evidenced by the quantities of gravel on the road and the noise of gravel chippings when driving over the surface. I can only see this as a waste of public funds, as the potholes will continue to degrade in bad weather.

title: Traffic Light Timing Issues
detail:
Traffic lights on the Upper Brook Street and Lower Brook Street side, the green lights are on for too short a time, Upper Church Street and Church Street side they are on for too long. I don't know if this is the case for all of the day but at 5:15 in the morning, you can barely get to the centre of the cross roads before they are turning red (5 seconds?) whereas Upper Church Street/Church street side is on for a least a minute, with no traffic going through them (maybe 1 car) meanwhile traffic has built up and if the car in front is slow to set off, you're back to a red light again.
